items.title: 9 people remain in critical condition after fatal train collision in U.K.
items.summary_auto: Nine people remain in a critical condition following a train crash on Friday near Bedford, about 100 kilometres north of London, in which the driver of one of the services was killed.

items.title: Bolivia's president declares emergency over blockade crisis, paving way to deploy military
items.summary_auto: Bolivia’s crisis intensified on Saturday as President Rodrigo Paz declared a state of emergency, enabling wider military deployment to clear blockades and restore order after protests brought the economy to ​a halt over the past 50 days.

items.title: Iran closes Strait of Hormuz, citing U.S., Israel ceasefire violations
items.summary_auto: Iran's top joint military command, Khatam al-Anbiya Central ‌Headquarters, said on Saturday that the ​Strait of Hormuz ​would be closed to vessel ​traffic, citing alleged violations of a ‌ceasefire agreement by the U.S. and Israel.

items.title: Should everyone be on GLP-1s? What science actually tells us (and doesn't) about the broader health benefits
items.summary_auto: The laundry list of possible health benefits from GLP-1 drugs seems to get longer by the day, from cancer prevention to combating addiction. But while there’s no question these medications show immense promise beyond their use for diabetes and weight loss, science is still catching up.

items.title: U.S. claims about higher drug trafficking from Canada don't match the data: fentanyl czar
items.summary_auto: As the U.S. claims its crackdown on drug trafficking at the border with Mexico is triggering more criminal activity from Canada, fentanyl czar Kevin Brosseau is pushing back — saying the numbers "really haven't borne that out."

items.title: The 2026 wedding trend: being yourself
items.summary_auto: For many young couples, the modern wedding has become an immersive experience through which their guests — and social media followers — can get a look into the unique tastes and personalities of the betrothed. When traditions are followed, they may be adapted to feel truer to the newlyweds.

items.title: After deadly protests, more Iranian women choosing to defy hijab law despite the dangers
items.summary_auto: Many women in Iran are defying the country's strict dress code by refusing to wear headscarves while out and about. Though several women told CBC News they still fear the authoritarian regime, others say deadly protests in January taught them they have nothing left to lose.

items.title: It started with chills. Weeks later, he was gone
items.summary_auto: Imagine working 36 years for your retirement, only for it to be cut short by a mosquito infected with West Nile virus. That is the tragic reality for an Ontario family speaking out, hoping to save lives as summer mosquito season gets underway.

items.title: A legal challenge opens a new front in the debate over Carney's climate agenda
items.summary_auto: Speaking at Trinity College in Ireland last weekend, Mark Carney recalled that Edmund Burke, the 18th-century politician and philosopher and an alumnus of Trinity, had once described society as a partnership between those who are living, those who are dead and those who are yet to be born.

items.title: There's backlash against the World Cup final's halftime show. Is it attacking tradition or just a good show?
items.summary_auto: The 2026 FIFA World Cup final will feature a halftime show for the first time. It has ruffled the feathers of some soccer fanatics, who see the move as a "Yankeefication" of the Beautiful Game.

items.title: How an Ontario man helped discover a lost Rembrandt, thought to be fake, but worth millions
items.summary_auto: Jonathan Bikker, a curator at the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam, led the effort to authenticate Vision of Zacharias in the Temple, a painting that had been dismissed by scholars in the 1960s and largely disappeared from public view.

items.title: Should everyone be on GLP-1s? What the science really tells us about cancer prevention and other benefits
items.summary_auto: The laundry list of possible health benefits from GLP-1 drugs seems to get longer by the day, from cancer prevention to combating addiction. But while there’s no question these medications show immense promise beyond their use for diabetes and weight loss, science is still catching up.

items.title: I always thought canes were fabulous — that is, until I needed one
items.summary_auto: Ev Bishop couldn't help but feel offended when people would notice her limping or struggling to move with her bad knee. But she couldn't accept that she had a disability, or might need a cane, until a visit from friends opened her eyes to how she was limiting herself.

items.title: How to move a beluga across the world
items.summary_auto: How does one transport an aquatic animal that can grow up to 4.5 metres and weigh up to 1,900 kilograms? Here’s a step-by-step guide.

items.title: Out-of-control wildfire near Lytton, B.C., prompts evacuation orders, Highway 1 closure
items.summary_auto: An out-of-control wildfire south of the village of Lytton, B.C., triggered evacuation orders and alerts late Friday.

items.title: American swimmer Kate Douglass breaks women's 50m freestyle world record
items.summary_auto: Kate Douglass broke the world record in the women's 50-metre freestyle at the TYR Pro Swim Series meet Friday night in Indianapolis.

items.title: Carney government passes law allowing authorization of banned pesticides
Australia confirms 1st mainland case of H5N1 bird flu
items.summary_auto: Australia will do all it can to curb any spread of H5N1 bird flu, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Saturday, as the  first mainland ‌infection was confirmed in a seabird a day after being detected in a remote southwestern region.

items.title: Canadian-Iranians respond to the U.S.-Iran peace deal with mixed reaction
items.summary_auto: Members of the Iranian diaspora in Toronto have mixed reactions to the U.S.-Iran peace deal, with some expressing relief that ordinary citizens will no longer live under active bombardment, but also skepticism that the new agreement will hold the Iranian government accountable.

items.title: Critics slam government plan to 'bail out' sagging condo sector in B.C.
items.summary_auto: A plan by the B.C. government and Ottawa to buy thousands of vacant condo units and turn them into affordable housing is drawing criticism. Some call it a "bail out" for developers.

items.title: New stadium needed to keep Whitecaps in Vancouver, says MLS commissioner
items.summary_auto: Major League Soccer commissioner Don Garber said before the United States beat Australia at the World Cup on Friday that the Vancouver Whitecaps need to have a new stadium deal in order to keep the club in Vancouver.

items.title: Wildfire that prompted evacuations in West Kelowna was caused by prohibited campfire, officials say
items.summary_auto: Officials have determined that a wildfire that prompted evacuation orders in West Kelowna, B.C., earlier this week was caused by a prohibited campfire inside a regional park.

items.title: Are thirsty soccer fans drinking all the beer in World Cup host cities?
K'omoks First Nation chief pushes back on Poilievre's opposition to treaty
items.summary_auto: Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre announced Friday that he and other Conservative party MPs will oppose the ratification of the K'omoks First Nation's treaty.

items.title: B.C. nurses reject tentative deal with province
items.summary_auto: Members of the B.C. Nurses' Union have voted to reject a tentative agreement with the province on Friday.

items.title: TV legend James Burrows, co-creator of Cheers and director for hit show Will & Grace, dies at 85
items.summary_auto: Celebrated televisions director and producer James Burrows, who was behind the hit show Cheers and directed thousands of episodes of hit shows like Friends and Will & Grace, has died at the age of 85.

items.title: Manitoba appoints 1st associate chief judge of reconciliation
items.summary_auto: A Manitoba provincial court judge is helping the court chart a new path forward as the province's first associate chief judge of reconciliation — a role she says she believes is the first of its kind in Canada.

items.title: 'This is our Serengeti': New study says more whales showing up in Vancouver waters
items.summary_auto: A  new study  says West Coast transient killer whales, also known as Bigg's killer whales, are now being seen in the inside waters of Vancouver Island for about 250 days a year on average.

items.title: Rabid bat found at Sask. daycare; school division apologizes for delay in telling parents
items.summary_auto: Children at a daycare in Spiritwood have been offered rabies treatment after a bat found inside the centre tested positive for the virus.

items.title: Ottawa adding $5.4B for child care to sustain national program over next 2 years
items.summary_auto: The federal government is giving provinces and territories an additional $5.4 billion over two years for the national $10-a-day child-care program to help with "cost pressures," Canada's families minister says.

items.title: Ukraine keeps striking Russian refineries and supply routes. How close is the country to an energy crisis?
items.summary_auto: Experts say Russia's energy industry is under increasing pressure as Ukraine tilts the war closer to the country that has been waging it, through a combination of long- and middle-range strikes.

items.title: Owner of beleaguered Toys 'R' Us Canada floats rebrand in court files
items.summary_auto: The owner of Toys "R" Us Canada is trying to buy some of the retailer's assets so it can keep the chain going or rebrand the beleaguered business that has been in creditor protection since February.

items.title: Driver dead, nearly 90 other people injured after passenger trains collide north of London
items.summary_auto: Two trains collided north of London on Friday, killing a driver and seriously injuring dozens of people on board.

items.title: Jelly Roll and Bunnie Xo break silence about divorce
items.summary_auto: American country star Jelly Roll and podcaster Bunnie Xo are breaking their silence following the  news earlier this week  of their divorce.

items.title: Canada imposes 10% global tariff on canned vegetables — excluding U.S., Mexico
items.summary_auto: Canada announced a 10 per cent tariff on imports of canned vegetables ‌on Friday, saying the measure, which excludes the United States, was aimed at addressing challenges facing its domestic ​producers.
